title: Deep Learning Basics
subtitle: How Neural Networks See, Hear, and Think!
overview: Deep Learning is the engine behind face recognition, voice assistants, self-driving cars, and generative AI. In this session, students explore how artificial neural networks are inspired by the human brain, how layers of neurons learn to recognise patterns, and how deep learning differs from traditional programming. Through visual demonstrations and hands-on experiments with Teachable Machine, students train their own neural network — no code required — and experience the magic of machine learning firsthand.

images._note: Add photos from the actual session — neural network diagrams, Teachable Machine screenshots, activation function graphs, student training demos.

keyTopics: Neural Network — A computational model loosely inspired by the brain, made of layers of interconnected artificial neurons that process data
Artificial Neuron — A mathematical function that takes multiple inputs, multiplies each by a weight, sums them, and applies an activation function
Weight — A number that determines how strongly one neuron influences another; weights are adjusted during training to improve accuracy
Activation Function — A mathematical function (ReLU, Sigmoid, Softmax) applied to the neuron's output to add non-linearity and decide if it 'fires'
Layers — Input Layer (receives raw data), Hidden Layers (extract features), Output Layer (produces the prediction or classification)
Deep Learning — Machine learning using neural networks with many hidden layers (deep = many layers); learns hierarchical features automatically
Training — The process of feeding labelled data through the network, comparing outputs to correct answers, and adjusting weights to reduce errors
Loss Function — A measure of how wrong the model's predictions are; training aims to minimise the loss (e.g. cross-entropy for classification)
Backpropagation — The algorithm that calculates how much each weight contributed to the error and adjusts them accordingly
Gradient Descent — The optimisation technique that iteratively adjusts weights in the direction that reduces the loss function
Epoch — One complete pass of the entire training dataset through the neural network; models typically train for many epochs
Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) — A specialised deep learning architecture for images; detects edges → shapes → objects layer by layer
Overfitting — When a model learns the training data too well (including noise) and performs poorly on new unseen data
Transfer Learning — Reusing a pre-trained model (trained on millions of images) and fine-tuning it for a new specific task — fast and efficient

ML for Kids — Train AI with Games and Stories

activity.title: Train Your Own Neural Network — Teachable Machine Challenge
parts: Computer or tablet with webcam and internet access
Various objects or hand poses for image classification
steps: Part 1 — Brain vs Computer: Discuss how the human brain recognises a cat — edges, fur, ears, whiskers — built up layer by layer. Show how a CNN does the same thing digitally. Draw a 3-layer neural network on the whiteboard together

Part 2 — TensorFlow Playground: Open playground.tensorflow.org. Students experiment with: changing the number of hidden layers, changing the number of neurons per layer, and observing how the decision boundary changes. Discuss: what does 'deeper' do to the model?
Part 3 — Teachable Machine Setup: Go to teachablemachine.withgoogle.com. Create a new Image Project. Define 3 classes (e.g. Thumbs Up, Thumbs Down, Open Hand — or Rock, Paper, Scissors)

Part 4 — Collect Training Data: For each class, hold up the gesture in front of the webcam and collect at least 50 images per class. Discuss: why do more varied examples (different lighting, angles) make a better model?
Part 5 — Train the Model: Click Train Model. Watch the training progress — loss decreasing, accuracy increasing over epochs. Discuss: what does the loss curve tell us about learning?
Part 6 — Test and Evaluate: Use the live Preview to test the model. Try edge cases — unusual lighting, partial gestures. Record accuracy. Discuss: where does it fail and why?
Part 7 — Overfitting Demo: Train a model with only 5 images per class and compare accuracy to the 50-image model. Discuss overfitting — the model memorises instead of generalising

Part 8 — Transfer Learning Discussion: Explain that Teachable Machine uses MobileNet (a pre-trained CNN trained on millions of images) and only fine-tunes the last layer on your data. This is why 50 photos is enough — transfer learning!

Challenge: Create a practical classifier — e.g. 'Mask / No Mask', 'Open Book / Closed Book', or 'Correct Posture / Slouching'. Train it, test it on classmates, and present accuracy results to the class

quiz.q: What is a Neural Network in AI?
options: A physical network of brain cells inside a computer chip
A computational model made of layers of interconnected artificial neurons that learn to recognise patterns from data
A type of firewall that protects AI systems from attacks
A network of computers connected to share training data
quiz.explanation: Neural networks are inspired by — but not identical to — the human brain! They are mathematical models with layers of connected nodes (neurons) that process inputs, apply weights, and pass results forward until a prediction is made at the output layer.

quiz.q: What is a Weight in a neural network?
options: The size of the dataset used for training
A number assigned to each connection between neurons that determines how strongly one neuron influences the next
The number of layers in the network
The speed at which the network processes data
quiz.explanation: Weights are the learnable parameters! Each connection between neurons has a weight. A high weight = strong influence; near-zero weight = almost no influence. During training, weights are repeatedly adjusted to make the network's predictions more accurate.

quiz.q: What does 'Deep' mean in Deep Learning?
options: The AI has a very deep understanding of human emotions
The neural network has many hidden layers — more layers allow the network to learn progressively more complex and abstract features
The training data is stored in a deep database
The network processes data more slowly and carefully
quiz.explanation: Deep = many hidden layers! A shallow network might have 1–2 hidden layers. A deep network has tens or hundreds. Each layer learns increasingly abstract features — early layers detect edges, middle layers detect shapes, deep layers detect objects or faces.

options: The function that starts the training process
A mathematical function applied to a neuron's output that introduces non-linearity, deciding whether and how strongly the neuron 'fires'
The function that loads training data into the network
A function that measures the accuracy of the model
quiz.explanation: Without activation functions, no matter how many layers you stack, the whole network would just be a linear equation — useless for complex patterns. ReLU (max(0,x)) is the most popular: it passes positive values through and zeros out negatives, adding the non-linearity needed to learn complex features.

quiz.q: What is Training a neural network?
options: Physically connecting neurons together with wires
Feeding labelled data through the network, comparing its predictions to the correct answers, and repeatedly adjusting weights to reduce errors
Writing the code that defines the network architecture
Testing the finished model on new data
quiz.explanation: Training is learning! The network sees thousands of labelled examples (e.g. photos labelled 'cat' or 'dog'), makes predictions, measures how wrong it was (loss), and uses backpropagation to adjust weights. After many passes (epochs), the network improves its predictions.

options: A function that removes unnecessary layers from the network
A mathematical measure of how wrong the model's predictions are — training aims to minimise this value
The memory lost when training data is deleted
A function that calculates how many neurons are inactive
quiz.explanation: Loss = how bad the model is right now! If the model predicts 'dog' with 95% confidence but it's actually a cat, the loss is high. If it correctly predicts 'cat' with 98% confidence, the loss is low. The entire training process is an effort to push the loss as close to zero as possible.

options: Running the network backwards to generate training data
The algorithm that calculates how much each weight contributed to the prediction error and adjusts all weights accordingly to reduce the loss
A technique for backing up the trained model to cloud storage
Propagating data backwards through the input layer
quiz.explanation: Backpropagation is the learning algorithm! After a forward pass produces an error, backprop works backwards through the network — calculating the gradient of the loss with respect to each weight. Weights that contributed more to the error get adjusted more. This is how the network learns.

quiz.q: What is an Epoch in neural network training?
options: A single neuron's prediction on one data sample
One complete pass of the entire training dataset through the neural network
A measurement of the network's accuracy on test data
The time it takes to train one layer of the network
quiz.explanation: One epoch = the network has seen every training example once! Most models train for many epochs (10, 50, 100+). With each epoch, the weights are refined and accuracy improves — you can watch the loss curve drop and accuracy rise on the training graph.

quiz.q: What is a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) specialised for?
options: Processing time-series data like stock prices
Generating text responses like a chatbot
Analysing and classifying images by detecting edges, shapes, and objects layer by layer
Translating languages
quiz.explanation: CNNs are the kings of computer vision! Convolutional layers slide small filters across the image to detect local features — first edges, then corners and curves, then shapes, then complex objects. This hierarchical feature detection makes CNNs incredibly powerful for image classification and object detection.

quiz.q: What is Overfitting in deep learning?
options: Adding too many training examples to the dataset
When the model learns the training data too well — including its noise and quirks — and performs poorly on new unseen data
When the network has too many layers to process efficiently
When the learning rate is set too high
quiz.explanation: Overfitting = memorising instead of generalising! An overfitted model scores 99% on training data but only 60% on new data. It learned the specific examples rather than the underlying pattern. Solutions: more training data, dropout layers, data augmentation, or simplifying the model.

quiz.q: What is Transfer Learning, and why is it powerful?
options: Transferring a trained model from one computer to another
Reusing a large pre-trained model (trained on millions of examples) and fine-tuning it for a new specific task — saving enormous amounts of time and data
Transferring knowledge from one student to another using AI
A method of moving training data between cloud servers
quiz.explanation: Transfer learning is a superpower! Training a deep network from scratch requires millions of images and days of GPU time. With transfer learning, you start with a model already trained on millions of images (like MobileNet or ResNet) and just fine-tune it on your small dataset. Teachable Machine uses exactly this approach!

quiz.q: In Google Teachable Machine, why do more varied training images (different lighting, angles, backgrounds) produce a better model?
options: More variety increases the file size which gives the AI more to process
Varied examples teach the model to recognise the CLASS (the gesture itself) rather than memorising irrelevant details like background colour or lighting, making it generalise better
The AI rewards users who collect more creative images
Variety reduces the number of epochs needed for training
quiz.explanation: Diversity = generalisation! If all your 'Thumbs Up' photos have the same bright background, the model might learn 'bright background' instead of 'thumbs up'. Varied lighting, angles, distances, and backgrounds force the model to focus on the actual gesture — making it robust to real-world variation.

options: The process of reducing the number of layers in a network
An optimisation algorithm that iteratively adjusts weights in the direction that reduces the loss function — like rolling downhill to find the lowest point
A method of visualising how data flows through the network
The descent of accuracy as training progresses
quiz.explanation: Gradient Descent finds the minimum loss! Imagine a hilly landscape where each point is a set of weights and the height is the loss. Gradient descent rolls downhill — adjusting weights in the direction that reduces loss most steeply. After many steps, it reaches the valley (minimum loss = best accuracy).

quiz.q: What are the three types of layers in a basic neural network?
options: Fast layer, medium layer, slow layer
Input layer (receives raw data), hidden layers (extract features), output layer (produces the final prediction)
Training layer, testing layer, validation layer
Convolutional layer, pooling layer, dropout layer
quiz.explanation: Three essential layers! Input layer = receives the raw data (pixels, numbers, words). Hidden layers = process and transform data, learning features at increasing levels of abstraction. Output layer = produces the final result — a class label, a number, or a probability distribution.

quiz.q: A student trains a Teachable Machine model with only 5 photos per class and finds it works perfectly on their specific training examples but fails on slightly different real-world cases. What has happened?
options: The model needs more epochs to train fully
The model has overfitted — it memorised the 5 specific examples rather than learning the general pattern of each class
The webcam resolution is too low for accurate classification
Transfer learning did not activate because the dataset was too small
quiz.explanation: Classic overfitting! With only 5 examples, the model memorised those exact photos instead of learning the general pattern. Solution: collect 50+ varied examples per class. More data, more variety = better generalisation to unseen real-world examples. This is why big datasets matter in deep learning.
